# Transcrição: Expanding The Unit - Key Relationships

- **Fonte:** Expanding The Unit - Key Relationships
- **Duração:** 07:26
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:01]

Hi, welcome to Goddus Project Practice Sessions. Today we're going to be talking about expanding the unit and key relationships. So in this practice session or real session, what you want to do is find out about the key people in your client's life. Now I normally take at least one session to do this and of course key people come up again in the coaching oftentimes over and over again. But it's really helpful to have this focus for an entire session because when we learn who all the players are in a person's life, just like in a story when we hear all the characters, we then have so many possible directions and ways to take our client that will give them greater joy and happiness. So and also of course understanding, we understand where they've come from, we understand the types of relationships that give them support and energy and the types of relationships that are hard for them. So when we when we learn, let's say that our client has a very challenging relationship with her in loss, for example, that never happens, right? Well when we learn this, we now not only understand and she's shared and she feels that trust and that relief of having told someone, we can also really take action to help her improve those relationships. And you can't do this with just your ideas of what in-laws are. You need to know about her in-laws, about the particular patterns and interactions, and this applies to every relationship. If you're going to help a mother improve her relationship with her teenage daughter, you need to know all you can about that teenage daughter. So when we learn about the key people in our client's life and we learn about the challenges with those people and we learn about the wonderful moments with those people, we have this wealth of information that we can draw on either in that session or in a future session to help the client deepen those relationships, improve those relationships, make changes that benefit not only the client but all the key people in the client's life. And sometimes this means bringing in new people into the client's world. Sometimes this means learning to create boundaries with some of the key people. One of the unique parts of strategic intervention is that, and this is a part, it's really a core belief, is that we never want to encourage a client to leave a relationship. Having said that, of course, if there's abuse of any kind, any kind of abuse or drug use or something that's harmful to our client, then sometimes we do need to help

## [03:01 - 06:03]

them leave a relationship. But in most mediocre to healthy relationships, what we want to do is help grow that relationship. And we do this through understanding the good relationships in our client's life as well as the not so good. We do this by learning what the key people in our client's life, what drives them, what they need, what their interactions are like. And sometimes this might mean that you actually have a back and forth with your client where you pretend to be one of those key people. Sometimes this means you actually call one of those key people on the phone and you invite them to a session. The beauty of strategic intervention is that we use systemic coaching, which means we bring in the client's whole system into the session. This can be done just through speaking to your client's key people and learning more and always thank them and elevate them for being part of your client's coaching experience. This could mean having sessions with your clients key people. This could mean just talking about them. Some clients need us to talk about those people for many months before they actually want to include them in the coaching. So I always suggest respecting your client's desires and rules around this. Unless there's a dramatic situation where you know you need to push a little bit to get one of those key people in or you might need to suggest that your client seek another kind of expert opinion. So if they're having big concerns about a teenager, let's say who's depressed, I would definitely suggest that you have a session with that teenager or that you bring in a psychologist's friend or a therapist to work with that teenager. So and whenever there's something going on in a family unit, you want to expand the unit to understand everyone's perspective. Not that your client is wrong, just that we all know there's many different views. So your client might be very worried about one of the children, but when you speak to her husband, he puts it in perspective and you learn that actually that child is doing great and so much better than they were doing three months ago. That will help you a lot in your coaching. But the main gist of today's practice session, because I took this to a big picture place, what I want you to remember today is learn about the key people. Get curious, discover everything you can, see if there's any patterns in your client's life with those key people, find out what those key people's main needs are in relationship with your client, find out what activities your client likes to do with those key people. Now some people think that there's this magic number six and nobody

## [06:03 - 07:26]

can really handle more than six deep relationships in their life. I don't really believe that, but I do think that the number six is a great number to focus on in conversation for one session. You know, everyone's different and some people have an enormous ability to hold many different relationships and some people have a beautiful ability to go really intense and deep with a few people in their life. And some people, their main most important relationship is with themselves. That's beautiful too. So I recommend that as a coach, you remember that what's most important is the client's happiness and fulfillment. It's never your rules or your beliefs about how many people we need to be in relationship with or how those relationships need to work or we all need to be this type of wife or this type of mother or this type of daughter. Find out what type of person relationship your client wants to be. That's really the heart of it and that's what will help you help your client to have the most fulfilling life. Okay, hope you have a great time with this the key people strategy.
